title: Big four making bank off Aussie dream of home ownership
text:
As mortgage-holders struggle to adjust to a third straight interest rate rise , new research reveals how much the banks make from customers chasing the Australian dream.

The Australia Institute says the big four banks - CBA, NAB, Westpac, and ANZ - rake in an average of $228,900 in profit over the 30-year span of an average $736,000 home loan.

Last year, those banks' profits rose to a collective $43 billion pre-tax, $16.9 billion of which was paid by owner-occupiers with a mortgage.

LIVE UPDATES: Saudi backlash forced Trump to back down ﻿

The big four banks are raking it in from home loans. (AAP Image/Joel Carrett)

The Institute found that mortgages for owner-occupiers made up 22.7 per cent of the big four banks' loans, they provided a "disproportionate" 39.3 per cent of their profits.

The banks also joined mining giants Rio Tinto and BHP as the top six most profitable companies in Australia.

Australia Institute co-chief executive Dr Richard Denniss said the figures were "obscene".﻿

READ MORE: Jeffrey Epstein suicide note released by New York court ﻿

"While so many Australians are going backwards, the banks' profits are only going in one direction – up," he said.

"In the first year of their mortgage, Australian homeowners are contributing more than $900 a month to their bank's profit."

The Institute also criticised the Reserve Bank's decision to lift interest rates to 4.35 per cent at Tuesday's board meeting.

Aussies are struggling under consecutive interest rises. (The Age)

﻿The increase saw interest rates hit their highest level in 15 years.

Senior economist Matt Grudnoff said the decision risked pushing the country into recession﻿, and that increasing interest rates would not reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

"All this does is heap more pain on already stretched households," he said.

"The only tool the RBA has to fight inflation is to change interest rates. But interest rates are ineffective at stopping inflation caused by supply shocks.﻿"

If Australia is nudged into recession, Grudnoff said, the RBA would be forced to rapidly lower interest rates again in an effort to stimulate the economy﻿, which would be a "humiliating backflip".

title: WA will fail to achieve net-zero by 2050, Woodside-funded report warns
text:
Western Australia will fail to achieve net-zero emissions by 2050 on its current trajectory, according to modelling commissioned by gas giant Woodside.

The report by Deloitte Access Economics found Australia's largest-ever gas project — Woodside's proposed multi-billion dollar offshore Browse facility — would help strengthen WA's energy security in the transition to net-zero, by providing a reliable source of energy as renewables are deployed.

But solar, wind and battery projects would need to be deployed at "unprecedented" rates in a sustained acceleration over decades to achieve the net-zero by 2050 target, with or without the Browse project, the report found.

"The Net Zero by 2050 without Browse scenario is highly ambitious relative to historical rates of renewable energy deployment," the report said.

"It will require a pace of change that is well beyond what has been delivered across the past decade."

The Deloitte modelling suggests WA would need to roll out renewables at 11 times historical rates to reach net-zero by 2050. (Supplied)

The modelling suggested without Browse, WA would need to roll out renewables at 11 times historical rates to reach net-zero by 2050 — a trajectory that "compresses what would have been decades of infrastructure development into a much shorter period".

"A renewable deployment rate five times larger than historical rates will only reduce emissions by circa 50 per cent by 2050, irrespective of whether Browse proceeds," the report said.

Deloitte used several key assumptions in its projections, including that emissions reductions were enforced by a cap on total emissions each year, and that the majority of the state's coal-fired electricity generation was phased out by 2030.

The report found Browse itself would not significantly change where WA's emissions would end up by 2050.

"In both scenarios, electrification and the transition to renewable electricity generation drive the energy transition," the report said.

Australia is bound by federal legislation to reach net-zero by 2050, but every jurisdiction except WA and the Northern Territory also has their own targets to reduce emissions before then.

Doubts over Woodside's Browse project Forget the environmental concerns. These financial analysts say economics will likely sink the project.

The ABC last week revealed the WA government was abandoning its own plans to introduce those interim targets.

Premier Roger Cook maintained his government was committed to the 2050 goal, but admitted WA's emissions may continue to rise in the short term.

But asked in parliament on Tuesday about when the state's emissions would begin to decrease to reach net-zero, cabinet minister Stephen Dawson said those projections were "cabinet in confidence".

Instead of the interim emissions targets, the state government is planning to introduce targets for renewable energy generation, carbon capture and storage, and green exports.

'Gas as additional energy source'

The Deloitte report said the scale of renewables rollout required to achieve net-zero by 2050 would have "significant delivery, coordination and system integration challenges".

"In this context, Browse gas provides an additional source of energy that helps mitigate these constraints," the report said.

Liz Westcott says the report highlights the Browse project's potential to support Australia's energy needs. (ABC News: Courtney Withers)

Woodside CEO Liz Westcott said the report highlighted Browse's potential to support the country's long-term energy needs.

"Browse is Australia's biggest undeveloped offshore gas resource and represents a major opportunity for the nation at a time where energy security matters more than ever," she said.

Woodside's Browse gas proposal explained Public consultation is opening on proposed changes Woodside says will minimise the environmental impact of its Browse gas plan. The Greens say the company is being "deceitful". This is what you need to know.

"Independent modelling shows Browse has the potential to power homes and businesses, support thousands of Australian jobs and generate significant revenue for governments while also helping to manage the risks and costs of the energy transition."

The Conservation Council of WA (CCWA) refuted the report's findings as a "smoke screen" designed to hide the Browse project's economic and environmental costs.

"Woodside has commissioned a report that shows WA is falling behind when it comes to climate action — and has taken all the wrong lessons from it," CCWA senior campaigner Greta Carroll said.

"Western Australia needs concrete emissions reduction targets and to prioritise renewables over new gas projects like Browse."

The report spoke to the scale of the Browse project, predicting the total capital investment would be about $48.7 billion in real terms over the project's lifetime.

The Deloitte report found Browse would provide $147 billion in total economic uplift over its lifetime, and pay about $56 billion in tax revenue.

The release of the modelling comes amid reports the federal environment department will make a decision on the Browse gas project before the end of this year.

title: Mum whose son died of meningococcal B welcomes free vaccines in Victoria
text:
Year 10 students will have access to free vaccinations for deadly meningococcal B as part of a government program rolling out from January 1 next year.

The government has allocated $9 million for the program, which will add the B strain to the existing meningococcal A,C,W and Y vaccines available for free under the National Immunisation Program (NIP).

Meningococcal B is a contagious bacterial infection that can cause serious injury and death, sometimes as quickly as within 24 to 36 hours.

Four cases of meningococcal disease have been reported in Victoria this year, with 18 cases reported in the past 12 months.

According to the Australian Institute of Health and Welfare, up to one in 10 patients with meningococcal disease will die and up to one in five surviving patients will suffer from long-term health problems.

Four other strains excluding the B strain are covered as part of the National Immunisation Program. (ABC News: Selina Ross)

RACGP Victoria chair Anita Muñoz welcomed the announcement, saying the vaccine provided vital protection for teenagers who were particularly at risk.

"I think in a country such as ours, a first world country, it's not acceptable for any adolescent to die of meningococcal B," she said.

" I don't feel comfortable with the notion that something as fundamental to our health system as vaccination should be determined by the disposable income of a child's parent. "

The vaccine, which was previously only available privately through GPs, can cost hundreds of dollars and requires multiple doses.

Symptoms of meningococcal disease: rash

fever

headache

nausea or diarrhoea

drowsiness

muscle and joint pain

neck stiffness

cold hands and feet

difficulty walking or talking Source: Meningitis Centre Australia

The cost has provided a barrier to vaccine uptake, even prompting one Ballarat mother to personally raise the money in 2024 to vaccinate an entire Year 11 class.

Dr Muñoz said infants were also at risk of the disease and called on the government to expand the funding of the programme to cover more cohorts.

"I think that we are halfway there. I would like the conversation to continue," she said.

Health Minister Harriet Shing said while the program was only funded for Year 10 students, it was exploring other funding avenues.

"We're continuing to advocate to the Commonwealth to consider adding this important vaccine to the NIP," Ms Shing said.

The government would also not confirm whether the vaccine program would continue beyond next year.

Other states, including South Australia and Queensland, currently provide free meningococcal B to eligible infants as well as teenagers.

Mother celebrates culmination of vaccination 'mission'

Monday's announcement took on special meaning for Melbourne mother Norliah Syer-Peterson, whose son, Levi, died suddenly last year after falling ill with meningococcal B.

Since then, Ms Syer-Peterson said she had channelled the pain of losing her son into a campaign for positive change.

"There is no way you could have ever imagined what that feels like," she said.

" The first thing I thought of was, 'I need to tell people about this because I don't want anybody else to go through this.' "

Norliah's son Levi died suddenly from meningococcal B after the rapid onset on illness last year. (Supplied)

Since his death in September, Ms Syer-Peterson said it had become her "mission" to educate parents about vaccination and push for meningococcal B vaccine funding.

In December, she delivered a petition with more than 41,000 signatures to parliament calling for the government to fund the meningococcal B vaccine.

"You don’t want your child to die in vain, but I know my child, and my child would want me to help work towards stopping other children from suffering a disability," she said.

Dr Muñoz paid tribute to Ms Syer-Peterson for her role in triggering the change.

"But no-one has been more instrumental in making this change than Norliah Syer-Peterson," she said.

"No-one should have to experience what Norliah and her family have. She made it clear how fast, brutal, and unforgiving meningococcal B is, and the government has responded."

Norliah Syer-Peterson has been praised for her advocacy for meningococcal B vaccination after her son, Levi, died. (Supplied)

Ms Syer-Peterson said she could not take credit for today's announcement, and said she was glad it had happened.

"It’s not just because of me. I’m not going to take credit for every single bit of advocacy that has been done in the past," she said.

title: Trees razed as North Adelaide golf course redevelopment gets underway
text:
Workers have begun cutting down trees at the North Adelaide Golf Course as part of a controversial redevelopment at the site.

Almost 600 trees are set to be removed from the area, north-west of the Adelaide CBD, for the golf course to host the LIV Golf and Australian Open tournaments.

Protesters filmed and held signs at the site as workers began felling the trees at Possum Park, along the River Torrens, late Monday morning.

Some attempted to stop vehicles from entering.

The South Australian government took control of the golf course from the Adelaide City Council two weeks ago, soon after speculation began about the future of LIV Golf.

The state government plans to spend $45 million to upgrade the golf course, which opened in 1905.

A protester holds a sign up as a contractor approaches the North Adelaide golf course. (ABC News: Che Chorley)

The Adelaide Park Lands Association organised a protest on Sunday that involved people tying yellow ribbons to some of the trees to be chopped down.

Association committee member Tim Jackson told 891 ABC Adelaide on Monday morning the protest could escalate.

"We certainly are considering more action and action of a different nature, but if we are to do that we want to make sure that our people are safe," he said.

Asked if that possibly meant putting their bodies on the line, Mr Jackson said, "Yes, that's possibly what might occur".

Protesters gather on Sunday to tie ribbons to trees at the North Adelaide golf course to oppose trees being cut down. (ABC News)

Mr Malinauskas told a press conference on Monday morning that it was "perfectly reasonable" for people to protest the redevelopment but they needed to comply with the law.

"As long as they do that, there's nothing to be concerned about," he said.

He said Labor took the work at the golf course to the March state election.

"We've now got the responsibility of developing on that commitment, which is what we're doing," he said.

"The plans are now being executed.

"We are seeing the tree removal occur just as we committed would be the case and, of course, they'll be replanted with at least three trees for every tree that gets removed."

Concept designs for the redevelopment of the North Adelaide golf course showing its proximity to the CBD. (Supplied: SA government)

Trees are also set to be removed in the park lands for the new Women's and Children's Hospital on Port Road and the MotoGP track to the east of the CBD.

The Adelaide round of LIV Golf was held at the Grange Golf Club from 2023 until this year and then will move to Kooyonga next year.

title: 'We can be a voice for women': Afghan cricketers push for ICC recognition
text:
Afghanistan's exiled women's cricket team has called on the International Cricket Council (ICC) to follow the lead of FIFA by recognising displaced players as eligible for international competition.

FIFA cleared the way for Afghani footballers, many of whom now reside in Australia, to return to international competition in April.

The team has not played an official competitive international since before the Taliban returned to power in 2021, with authorities imposing sweeping restrictions on women and girls, including restrictions affecting education, work and sport, forcing many female athletes to flee the country or simply abandon competition.

Now, Canberra-based Afghan women's player Shafiqa Khan hopes cricket will follow suit, telling ABC Sport she felt "very proud and emotional" upon hearing about FIFA's landmark decision.

Shafiqa Khan has urged the ICC to emulate FIFA's decision to recognise her displaced team. (ABC News: Simon Beardsell)

"FIFA has given significant hope to many Afghan women athletes and shown that no matter where they live, they can represent their country and follow their passion," Khan said.

"It [would] mean a lot and it would be an unforgettable moment for all Afghan women who live back home and are denied their basic rights.

"We can be a voice for Afghan women.

"We can represent Afghan women who live back home and be a voice for a million girls who are denied their basic rights and follow their passion."

Last year, the ICC announced the creation of a monetary fund and task force to support the displaced Afghan women's cricket team.

The statement said the ICC would partner with the cricket boards of Australia, England and India to provide "meaningful support" to Afghan women.

Dr Catherine Ordway (centre) was instrumental in the Afghan women's cricket team escaping the Taliban. (ABC News: Donal Sheil)

However, Dr Catherine Ordway noted that task force, the second established by the ICC, has only met once and has even resisted calls for a representative of the Afghan women's team to be included.

"It's absolutely not a good look," Dr Ordway, a sport integrity expert and visiting scholar at UNSW'S School of Business Canberra, told ABC Sport.

"There's an example already out there with the international federation for football [FIFA], and I hope that other international federations will follow suit, including the cricketers.

‘Justice is served’ for Afghan women’s football team A FIFA rule change has allowed the Afghan women's football team to compete in official internationals for the first time since the Taliban took over in 2021.

"The ICC has put together a task force … but they do not have a plan as far as we can see.

"At the moment it's just made up of the Indian cricket, English cricket and Australian cricket representatives but none of the women are represented and they do not have a plan going forward after the funding ends in August.

"That's really concerning for the women's team.

"They want to send a strong message to the women and girls stuck inside Afghanistan who are not allowed to get educated, can't work, can't move freely, much less play cricket. They want to send a strong message that women can do anything but the ICC just hasn't stepped up to the plate yet."

Since the Taliban takeover, there has been considerable controversy surrounding the Afghan men's team's continued involvement in international cricket, with ICC regulations requiring all full member nations to have a national women's team.

Cricket Australia has twice postponed limited overs bilateral series between the two nations due to human rights concerns.

However, the countries have played each other as part of ICC tournaments during that time, and Afghanistan retains its status as a Test-playing nation.

Dr Catherine Ordway was the first person to contact the Afghan cricket team in 2021. (ABC News: Donal Sheil)

As cricket prepares to make its return to the Olympics in LA, it is unclear how Afghanistan's attitude to women's cricketers fits within the International Olympic Committee's Olympic Charter.

The Olympic Charter states that "the practice of sport is a human right" and that "every individual must have access to the practice of sport, without discrimination of any kind in respect of internationally recognised human rights".

The charter goes on to state that "discrimination of any kind" is contrary to the Fundamental Principles of Olympism.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Dr Ordway said it was important to keep the plight of the Afghanistan women front of mind for the ICC.

She hopes a planned trip to the UK for the players that coincides with the ICC's Annual Conference in Edinburgh, Scotland, from July 8 to 11 can help on that front.

"At the moment, the women's team isn't even on the agenda for the ICC," Dr Ordway said.

"The fact that they are going to be there at the same time, I am really hopeful [it] will help to put some pressure on the ICC to make some positive decisions, both for this group of displaced women but [also] for the ones inside Afghanistan as well.

"It's really important to send a strong message that the Taliban's message on gender apartheid is completely unacceptable."

The ICC has been contacted for comment.

title: India tells citizens to shun gold, cut fuel use amid energy crisis
text:
Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi has appealed to the country's 1.4 billion citizens to work from home, avoid travel and stop buying gold, as the Iran War continues to send energy costs soaring.

Mr Modi on Sunday called for a return to work-from-home arrangements and online meetings, measures adopted during the COVID-19 pandemic.

He also urged people to use public transport, and carpool wherever possible.

"In the current situation, we must place great emphasis on saving foreign exchange," he said.

Last month, he said there was no proposal to raise pump prices for diesel and gasoline, leaving it among the countries yet to raise prices despite the global surge.

People on their motorbikes wait for their turn to get fuel at a petrol station, in Karachi, Pakistan. (Reuters: Akhtar Soomro)

Mr Modi asked citizens to cut non-essential travel, calling for collective restraint to help protect India's economy during the deepening energy crisis.

He urged people to avoid buying gold, which India spends heavily on during weddings

He has pushed for the adoption of electric vehicles and asked farmers to halve their use of chemical fertilisers and for families to reduce cooking oil consumption, describing that move as both healthy and patriotic.

South Asia bears the brunt

India is not alone in feeling the pressure.

Governments across Asia are scrambling to insulate their economies from the ongoing energy crisis.

India has asked their farmers to cut down on fertilizer use. (Reuters: Jayanta Dey)

Overall oil imports to Asia, which take 85 per cent of Gulf crude shipments, fell sharply in April following nearly two months of disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.

Governments across South Asia have been spending billions of dollars on subsidies and import duty waivers in an attempt to shield consumers from the worst of the price rises.

The economies of Pakistan, Bangladesh and Sri Lanka are considered the most vulnerable.

Pakistan is reportedly paying about $30 million above pre-war market rates to secure liquefied natural gas supplies after its supply was cut off from Qatar.

Pakistan is aggressively shopping for liquefied natural gas to combat a severe power crisis. (Reuters: Jose Cabezas)

Analysts say the vulnerability of these nations is driven in part by their heavy reliance on state-subsidised energy.

"These countries use more of their resources on subsidising domestic public energy enterprises and basically shielding the final consumers from the energy price shock," an economist at S&P's Asia-Pacific Economics unit told the Reuters news agency.

Buying more from Russia

Indonesia, South-East Asia's largest economy, is looking to buy oil from Africa and Latin America and has plans to buy 150 million barrels from Russia by the end of the year.

Russia is also considering sending its first oil product cargo to Laos, according to Russia's Deputy Prime Minister on Saturday.

Vietnam, Thailand, the Philippines and Sri Lanka are among the countries that have been actively seeking Russian oil as they suffer shortages.

Asia countries are buying more oil from Russia due to the Middle East crisis. (Reuters via Sputnik/Gavriil Grigorov)

In Thailand, a state-owned refiner is reported to have paused crude purchases after national output of refined products stepped up, following a government ban on exports.

Japan, which sources about 95 per cent of its oil from the Middle East, has purchased more from the United States.

It is paying spot market prices plus significantly higher shipping costs, given that American supplies take roughly twice as long to arrive as those from the Gulf.

Reuters/AFP

title: A billionaire mined their land without permission. Now judgment day is looming
text:
It has been portrayed as a David and Goliath battle: a group of Aboriginal elders from a remote community in Western Australia's outback versus one of the richest and most prominent men in Australia.

And tomorrow's historic Federal Court decision, in which Justice Stephen Burley will reveal how much compensation the Indigenous group will receive, is certainly not without drama.

Andrew Forrest backed a splinter group of Yindjibarndi elders. (ABC News: Michelle Stanley)

On one side is flamboyant mining billionaire Andrew Forrest, known simply as "Twiggy" to many Australians, whose company FMG has been mining vast quantities of iron ore on land sacred to the Yindjibarndi people in WA's Pilbara since 2013.

Without their permission.

On the other side is the Yindjibarndi Aboriginal Corporation (YAC), headed by Michael Woodley, who has been pivotal in his people's struggle to both be recognised for their deep connection to the land that stretches back tens of thousands of years and to be compensated for FMG's actions.

Michael Woodley has been pivotal in his people's struggle. (ABC Pilbara: Amelia Searson)

Recognition came in 2017, when a court awarded the Yindjibarndi exclusive native title rights over a 2,700-square-kilometre area.

It was a claim fought by FMG every step of the way and through multiple expensive appeals.

Compensation will be determined tomorrow, but exactly who has to pay and how much could have consequences for mining operations and the conduct of governments across the country for decades to come.

Ancient history

This story begins not in 2017, or 2013, but about 50,000 years ago when Aboriginal people first settled in the Pilbara.

Evidence of their occupation, in the form of archaeological sites and rock carvings, are scattered throughout the region and helped form the basis of the native tile determination.

Yindjibarndi country near Millstream in WA's Pilbara. (ABC Pilbara: Amelia Searson)

It was not until the early 1860s, more than 30 years after the Swan River colony was established by the British at what is now Perth, that the people of the Pilbara came into contact with white settlers keen to establish cotton plantations.

Violent and bloody confrontations were to follow in the years to come, including the notorious Flying Foam Massacre at the Burrup Peninsula, as the settlers sought to establish the colony and develop a burgeoning resources industry.

Yindjibarndi country before FMG built its Solomon Hub mines. (Supplied: Phil Davies, Juluwarlu/Yindjibarndi Aboriginal Corporation)

Pastoral stations were carved out across Yindjibarndi country and Aboriginal people rounded up to be used as indentured labour.

Just as they had done across the empire, the British had no qualms about dispossessing the native people of the Pilbara and seizing land they considered belonged to no-one.

Yindjibarndi elder and artist Wendy Hubert on country. (Supplied: Philip Davies, Juluwarlu Art Group)

It would be more than a century later, in 1992, that the archaic concept of "terra nullius" was finally extinguished by the High Court in its seminal Mabo judgement that established that Aboriginal people had prior rights to the land.

Stalemate

In 2003 the Yindjibarndi lodged the native title claim that would eventually take another 14 years to be determined.

But FMG was not prepared to wait.

By that time Mr Forrest's company had already begun clearing ore-rich land across the Yindjibarndi claim area that it planned to develop into a series of mines, destroying ancient heritage sites in the process.

Other miners in the area, including Rio Tinto, had negotiated Indigenous land use agreements with traditional owners, under which compensation was paid, although these failed to stop its infamous destruction of Juukan Gorge.

FMG initially sought to do the same.

Michael Woodley on country at the Fortescue mine site in 2008 before construction began. (Supplied: Phil Davies, Juluwarlu/Yindjibarndi Aboriginal Corporation)

Negotiations with the YAC began in 2007 and the Yindjibarndi were keen to also establish a heritage agreement with the mining company to protect culturally important sites.

But no agreement could be reached.

Divide and conquer

Instead, Mr Forrest began secretly organising and backing a breakaway group of Yindjibarndi people, who then formed the Wirlu-Murra Yindjibarndi Aboriginal Corporation.

Mr Forrest wanted the group to replace the YAC as the native title claimants and offered them $4 million a year in royalties compensation.

It was far below the 5 per cent the YAC had sought, which would have amounted to about $68 million a year at the time.

Barnkwirnanha, a traditional navigation marker between Yindjibarndi and Eastern Guruma, close to the Fortescue mine. (Supplied: Phil Davies, Juluwarlu/Yindjibarndi Aboriginal Corporation)

The Federal Court ruled in 2013 that YAC was the only organisation that could sign land use agreements in the area.

The division caused by the formation of the rival group has never healed, and testimony to the court in the compensation case laid bare the ongoing anguish and scars borne by the community.

Michael Woodley, TJ (deceased) and Charley Cheedy performing ritual at the Ganyjingarringunha wurndu (creek) in 2011, before construction of the Fortescue mine. (Supplied: Phil Davies, Juluwarlu/Yindjibarndi Aboriginal Corporation. Permission was granted to publish this photo)

Elder Lyn Cheedy was among many who spoke of the irreparable damage caused not only by FMG's mining operations on her people's country, but also by the split.

"We have been torn apart. It makes me really sad," she said.

Mining begins anyway

Mr Forrest did not let the absence of an agreement with traditional owners get in the way of his iron ore dream, and neither did the federal and state governments.

In 2013 FMG officially began digging up ore at its Firetail mine, on Yindjibarndi country, having gained the necessary government approvals to do so.

Part of FMG's Solomon Hub mining operations in WA's Pilbara. (Supplied: Phil Davies, Juluwarlu/Yindjibarndi Aboriginal Corporation)

No-one, it seemed, was prepared to wait for the Federal Court to assess the Yindjibarndi's claim, not when there were billions of dollars in ore to be dug up and shipped to China.

And when the court did finally make its determination in 2017, ruling that the Yindjibarndi were in fact the rightful and exclusive owners of the land, FMG again failed to negotiate a land use agreement with them.

Instead, it tried to appeal the native title decision all the way to the High Court, which was ultimately refused.

Landmark decision

The Federal Court set-up at the remote Bangkangarra campsite. (ABC Pilbara: Ted O'Connor)

Tomorrow's Federal Court decision may end up being the biggest native title compensation package in Australia's history.

It is only the third determination for damages ever made and follows a ruling in February that the NT government pay more than $54 million in compensation to the Gudanji, Yanyuwa, and Yanyuwa-Marra peoples after Glencore's McArthur River mine was built on their country.

The Yindjibarndi case could easily eclipse this figure, with YAC seeking one per cent of the production value profit of the mines on its land, as well as compensation for the loss of around 250 cultural sites.

That could add up to more than $1 billion.

Federal Court Justice Stephen Burley (centre) welcomed to Yindjibarndi country at Bangkangarra. (Supplied: Phil Davies, Juluwarlu/Yindjibarndi Aboriginal Corporation)

Who has to pay up will be a keenly watched part of the decision.

Is it FMG? Or is the state government on the hook for granting Mr Forrest's company mining approval in the first place?

Should the amount be based on royalty payments or on land value?

And how do you put a price on the cultural value that has been lost?

For Yindjibarndi man Charlie Cheedy, whose grandfather gave extensive evidence in the first native title case, he hopes the decision will ultimately carry on his elders' legacies.

Charlie Cheedy gave evidence during the court process. (ABC Pilbara: Mietta Adams)

"It's been a sad journey for me because my teachers, my grandfather, had a big part of it," he said.

"He was there from day one … and that's why this battle was important to me."

He said that dedication to protect country has been passed to the younger generations.

Charlie's son Ashton Cheedy practising culture on country. (Supplied: Charlie Cheedy)

"They're on this journey with us, they want to fight this battle with the mining company," Mr Cheedy said.

"Whatever happened back in the past, leave it back there. Tomorrow's a new day."

Justice Burley will hand down his decision in the Federal Court at 2pm WST on Tuesday.

title: Herbert wins maiden LIV title, secures US Open spot
text:
Australian golfer Lucas Herbert has held his nerve to claim his maiden LIV Golf League title and secure a ticket to next month's US Open in New York.

Herbert had a five-shot final-round lead slashed to one before regaining his composure to see off the challenge of Spanish great Sergio García in a dramatic climax to LIV Golf Virginia.

Too ill to complete a full practice round before the big-money event, Herbert showcased all his character and grit in closing with a roller-coaster three-under-par 69 to win by four shots and collect a life-changing $US4 million ($A5.54 million) winner's cheque.

The Bendigo product finished at 24 under for the 72 holes, with García (70) edging out big-hitting American Bryson DeChambeau (66) by a shot to snare the runner-up spoils at Trump National Golf Club in Washington, DC.

Teammates Cameron Smith, Marc Leishman and Elvis Smylie showered Herbert with champagne on the 18th green after the 30-year-old's wire-to-wire triumph completed a full set of LIV Golf individual victories for the all-Australian Ripper GC outfit.

Leishman jagged his maiden title last year in Miami before Smylie held off Jon Rahm down the stretch to score a thrilling one-shot victory at LIV Golf Riyadh in February.

Ripper skipper Smith is a three-time LIV winner but, ironically, his most recent victory came before his three teammates' breakthroughs, three years ago in New Jersey.

With his seventh professional victory, Herbert has also now won on all four major worldwide circuits, having previously saluted on the US PGA Tour, Europe's DP World Tour and in Australasia.

Lucas Herbert is off to the US Open in New York next month. (Getty Images: Tasos Katopodis)

"Probably that I can perform pretty damn well when things aren't perfect," Herbert said when asked what he had learnt about himself.

"I was pretty sick all week and I woke up this morning probably feeling worse than I did the last few days.

"I had Sergio coming at me for 36 holes really hard and he pushed me the whole way, made me earn that one."

Despite the end margin, Herbert's success came anything but easily.

With four birdies in his first eight holes, he was cruising after building a five-shot advantage.

But after overcooking his nine iron on the par-3 ninth and needing three chip shots to find the green, Herbert racked up a dreaded double bogey as García drained a 30-foot birdie putt in a three-shot swing.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Herbert was suddenly clinging to a two-stroke lead heading to the back nine.

The buffer was just one when García conjured a tap-in birdie at number 10.

But there was a further twist at the 11th, when Herbert holed a testing 12-foot downhill putt before García missed his shorter birdie attempt to give the Australian some breathing space, two shots back in front.

He was four clear again after picking up the sixth birdie of his round on 12 and García bogeyed 13, before slamming the door shut on the Spaniard with another birdie at 15.

"I didn't help myself," Herbert said.

"I missed a few putts here and there and made it a contest late, but after the rain delay, the way I played those eight shots, I'm so proud of that and can't wait to celebrate with everyone tonight."

AAP

title: 'I can't believe we did this': The joy of having babies in your 40s
text:
Not long after giving birth to her son, Heather was asked by a stranger at a restaurant: Are you his mum or grandma?

Heather had given birth at 47. She says it was a long but worthwhile wait.

"Once we were home [from hospital] I thought, 'I can't believe you're mine. I can't believe we did this,'" she said.

"There was nowhere else that I wanted to be, I waited so long for him. I was so in love with him."

Being an older mum comes with many joys, Heather says, but also judgement, which was apparent when quizzed at the restaurant that day.

Whether it's about investing in careers early, wanting to be financially stable, infertility or waiting to meet "the right person", Australian women are increasingly having children later in life.

Before 1991, only 5 per cent of first-time mothers were over the age of 35. By 2020, that figure increased to 17 per cent.

ABC podcast Ladies, We Need to Talk spoke with Heather and Zoe about what it's like to be an older mum.

I embrace the messiness of motherhood

Heather, 49, Perth

Heather always wanted to be a mum. (Supplied)

When Heather was 19, she was diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), a hormonal disorder that can affect fertility.

She was told getting pregnant would be difficult.

Despite this, Heather always hoped to have children. Her husband Ryan — who she met at 26 — took a little longer to get on board.

After some health challenges in her 30s, the couple started trying for a baby, and at 45, Heather was finally pregnant.

But on her first visit to the doctor, she was told the pregnancy wouldn't be viable.

"[The doctor] said, 'I will expect you to miscarry this baby in the next week or two.' And I did," she said.

Heather was heartbroken. And the loss clarified for her husband that he really wanted to be a dad, after all.

Listen to the ripsode Yumi Stynes meets two first time mums who fell pregnant in their mid-40s to talk about the challenges and the joys of being an older mum.

A year later, Heather was pregnant for the second time.

The pregnancy was tracking well, but as a 47-year-old diabetic, Heather said it was high-risk and meant lots of hospital visits.

On top of managing her own health in pregnancy, Ryan was diagnosed with a rare cancer.

She said it was a difficult time. But when their son Will was born, relief washed over her.

"He just screamed at me bloody murder. I was just like, 'Yes, he's OK.'"

Despite occasional judgement, Heather said age didn't stop her from being deeply involved and active with her son.

"I'm old enough to be the mum of the mums at the park. But I'm the one that's up doing the activities with the kids," she said.

" I'm on the flying fox with him on my knee, going down screaming like a banshee, having fun with him. "

Heather said age and life experience allowed her to appreciate those moments.

"I realise that memories are what matters most," she said.

"I'm not going get that time back, he's not going to get that time back. I need to invest in him."

She said maturity also allowed her to have a more relaxed approach to the messiness of motherhood and let little things go.

Health benefits linked to later age pregnancy There are several evidence-based advantages associated with late-age births — for both mum and bub — and experts say we should be talking about them more.

Ryan's cancer is terminal, and his health means a lot of the parenting falls to Heather, but they're cherishing it all.

"Thankfully he's got a fairly slow-growing cancer. So he should be able to last a fair few years and hopefully old enough for Will to have memories of him in person rather than just looking at photos and videos," Heather said.

"Even knowing that Ryan was going to get sick, even knowing that I would be probably raising him on my own, I would do it again in a heartbeat.

"The small moments — the family moments together — just are the best moments of my life."

How have you found becoming a mum later in life? We'd love to hear from you.

I was ready to be a mum

Zoe, 47, Sydney

Zoe had 13 IVF cycles before she got pregnant with her daughter. (Supplied)

Zoe was 44 and her husband was 51 when they had their daughter.

She sometimes reads judgemental things online about older mums, such as: "You're not going to be around for your child" or "you're going to be the oldest mum in school".

"So what?" says Zoe. "You've worked harder than anything in your entire life to get this child into the world. It doesn't matter how old you are."

And Zoe did work hard. By the time she was pregnant with her daughter, she had been through 13 IVF cycles.

She had also miscarried twins the year prior.

"It was really devastating. Just to kind of have that excitement and then to be told that it's probably not going to happen."

Sign up to the ABC Lifestyle newsletter Get a mid-week boost and receive easy recipes, wellbeing ideas, and home and garden tips in your inbox every Wednesday. You’ll also receive a monthly newsletter of our best recipes.

After that loss, Zoe threw everything at getting pregnant again.

"I changed my diet. I went to acupuncture every week. I did yoga every night. I meditated."

When it eventually happened, she was nervous.

"You're just praying and you're wishing … please stay baby. Please stay."

Zoe was nervous throughout her pregnancy. (Supplied)

Her daughter was born seven weeks early and weighed just 1.2 kilograms. After six weeks in NICU, they could bring their little baby home.

"After everything we'd been through and everything that happened, I was like: 'I got her'.

"I was so focused on her that first year. I did have extended maternity leave.

" I was able to just be immersed in being her mum. "

Zoe says she's been able to take time off to focus on her daughter. (Supplied)

Zoe is now dealing with the toddler years while deep in perimenopause.

"I feel like I'm losing it like a lot of the time. I feel like, 'Am I premenstrual? Or am I just like hormonal?'"

She says the best thing about being an older mum is feeling like she hasn't missed out on anything.

"I've travelled, I've had careers — those things that I think I saw a lot of my friends go through where it was like, 'Oh, we can't come out.'

"[I was] still going out in [my] 30s … having fun."

title: Regional Victorians flip national flag upside down in protest at 'neglect'
text:
When you drive the Murray Valley Highway as it stretches east to west along the Victoria-NSW border, Australian flags hung upside down feature prominently on front lawns and in paddocks.

For some of the farmers who work this land, it is their way of showing their disapproval of the state and federal governments.

Doug Fehring is flying an upside-down flag at his property in northern Victoria in protest against government policies. (ABC News: Tyrone Dalton)

Doug Fehring is one of those Victorians flying an upside-down Australian flag outside his Leitchville property.

He said he was fed up with "a lack of political will" to listen and respond to what Australians think about water management, cost of living, renewable energy projects, and food and fuel security.

"For all the submissions, meetings and protests we've done, nothing's happened, no changes, and I'm a bit tired of it," Mr Fehring said.

"They're not listening, and we've got further buybacks being hijacked by the Greens, the Liberals are falling apart, the Coalition can't control itself either — it's just a shambles really, and we've got nowhere to go.

"And that's why people are going to One Nation, really."

Former farmer Lloyd Polkinghorne lives in Koondrook, and he's noticed a growing number of upside-down flags displayed on properties in the region.

Irrigator Lloyd Polkinghorne has become dissatisfied with politicians. (Supplied: Lloyd Polkinghorne)

He said the international sign of maritime distress is emerging for "several reasons".

"One of the big ones that's facing our farmers is water buybacks ... [but] we've also got power lines ... and then we've got the burden of fuel and fertiliser pricing," Mr Polkinghorne said.

It's not the first time rural Victorians have used flags to show their discontent with governments.

In 2020, farmers in northern Victoria flew the Eureka flag, historically used by miners to rebel against gold mining taxes in the Victorian gold rush, to protest against water policy and the closure of hundreds of dairy farms.

Push to One Nation

Leigh, who lives in western Victoria and didn't want to give his last name, spent 17 years in the Australian Army before being medically discharged five years ago.

He described those displaying upside-down flags as brave, and said the move was "fairly understandable".

"There is a lot of distress happening in our community at the moment," he said.

"It’s extremely sad to see what’s happening in Victoria.

"There is so much hardship at the moment, and it's evident everywhere you go in the state, and I think it’s only getting worse.”

Leigh said he had noticed a shift in attitudes towards One Nation in his circle as it got tougher for regional Australians.

"When I was a kid, going back 25 to 30 years ago, Pauline Hanson was nothing but somewhat of a joke," he said.

" But now … I don't know any person in my circle at the moment that isn't for the whole One Nation movement. "

Some rural Victorians are flying Australian flags upside down to signal their dissatisfaction with government. (ABC News: Warwick Long)

Nigel Hicks is from Wyuna in Victoria's Goulburn Valley and said flying flags out the front of properties was a way for time-poor farmers to protest.

"You've got ministers and government people driving along these roads to go to some of these meetings, and it's one way of people permanently showing where they stand," he said.

"I think we saw that even with the sets of CFA overalls hanging on farmers' gates [over the Victorian government's emergency services levy]."

An issue of neglect

Political sociologist and associate professor Josh Roose said the upside-down flag movement reflected a strong sense of "disempowerment" in the community.

Deakin University extremism researcher Josh Roose. (ABC News: Kyle Harley)

"This is, more broadly speaking, an issue of regional Australians and their neglect over a period of decades," Dr Roose said.

"Where the infrastructure has been hired out, where people can no longer necessarily buy a house in the town they grew up in due to external investment, Airbnbs and so on ... diesel is the lifeblood of our regions and even that is becoming unaffordable."

Dr Roose said the perceived disempowerment in the regions coincided with a rise in populist parties.

For the first time, One Nation is polling above 20 per cent nationally and is ahead of the combined vote of the Liberal and National parties.

He said One Nation's framing was "certainly populist", appealing to people's feeling of being "neglected [and] left behind", but he noted the party was "not particularly heavy on solutions".

Dr Roose said it coincided with a rise in the sovereign citizen movement, which similarly stemmed from a distrust in government and a sense of marginalisation.

An upside-down national flag is historically known as a maritime distress signal. (ABC News: Warwick Long)

'An American thing'

Ralph Kelly is a vexillologist — someone who studies flags — and is president of Flags Australia.

He said flying a flag upside down historically meant a ship was in distress, but it had been repurposed by protest groups.

"The modern use of the upside-down flag is really an American thing since the Trump MAGA movement, when many Americans [used it] in protests [and] street demonstrations," he said.

Ralph Kelly says the upside-down flag has been claimed by protest groups unhappy with governments. (Supplied: Ralph Kelly)

Mr Kelly said sovereign citizens had also claimed the red ensign flag as part of their political protest against the government.

"They're saying, 'It's not the government flag, we don't support the government, so we're not flying the government's flag,'" he said.

"And some of those sovereign citizen people are flying it upside down as an extra message of protest and disagreement with government policies."

This sign in a paddock displays the phrase "Make Australia Great Again". (ABC News: Warwick Long)

'Getting the economy working for people'

Dr Roose said the climate in the regions could make work dangerous for government enforcement agencies, such as the police.

"We’ve got to move to a point where ideally we can take the temperature out of the environment, but a big part of that is getting the economy working for people," he said.

"We are at a pretty important inflection point here in Australia, and there needs to be a national conversation about supporting our regions far more effectively than has been done."

But not all regional residents feel the same way as their inverted-flag-flying neighbours.

Michael Purcell from Acheron, north-east of Melbourne, told ABC Radio Statewide Drive his ex-military friends in particular found the flying of the Australian flag upside down disrespectful.

"I think there are ways and means of making a statement," Mr Purcell said.

"As far as disrespect to the Australian flag, I do have a problem with that."

Another listener, Michael from Wooragee, said he found the flags useful to identify who he would stay away from.

"I love how the village idiots now mark their location with a red ensign," he said.

"It's very helpful to know who to avoid."

title: Season-defining VAR call causes division in EPL
text:
Arsenal remains in control of the Premier League title race, thanks to a late goal and an even later, dramatic VAR call on Sunday.

A 1-0 win over relegation-threatened West Ham restored Arsenal's five-point lead over second-place Manchester City and came with a huge dose of controversy.

Leading thanks to Leandro Trossard's 83rd-minute goal, Arsenal looked like it would be throwing away two crucial points — and losing its slim advantage in the title race — when West Ham substitute Callum Wilson lashed a shot over the line following a corner in the fifth minute of stoppage time.

Arsenal's players complained and there was a long video review, which spotted West Ham player Pablo had his left arm across the face of David Raya as the Arsenal goalkeeper attempted to get to the ball after the corner was swung in.

Raya flapped weakly at the ball amidst the general melee that every corner kick has descended into this season, with players from both sides hanging off each other and grabbing each other's shirts — Pablo himself was being held by three separate Arsenal players.

Chris Kavanagh faced an unenviable task in unpicking the madness. (Getty Images: Marc Atkins)

The referee, Chris Kavanagh, looked at the incident on the pitch-side monitor and chose to rule out the goal, sparking delirium on one side of the divide and deep anger on the other.

"I saw the replay now and I think it's an obvious decision," Arsenal manager Mikel Arteta said in an interview with the BBC.

"It was a massive call but it was clearly the right call.

"My first instinct was foul but then the time goes by, the referee is waiting for the VAR decision — but when you look at it, the contact affects the way David Raya lifts his hands, the way he catches the ball. It is almost on his throat.

"Massive decision, especially when the two teams are fighting for such big things."

West Ham's players felt aggrieved, with Tomáš Souček complaining that video reviews were only brought in to look for "big errors".

"For football, this is a disappointing moment and for every fan this is not what we want," Souček said.

"We want goals, we want celebrations. Not to wait 10 minutes for a small foul."

West Ham United's players were furious at the decision. (Getty Images: PA Images/John Walton)

West Ham captain Jarrod Bowen said "contact and tussles" were expected in the EPL.

"I get that you can't wipe a goalkeeper out and there's arms there but the 'keeper's come out to try and grab the ball so … he's got to expect some contact," he said.

"It's a corner kick, it's physical, it's the Premier League — there's going to be contact and tussles."

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Hammers coach Nuno Espírito Santo bemoaned the lack of consistency over the season.

"I'm going to see it now and try to understand it better," Nuno told the BBC.

"I am not here to judge the work of the referee or the work of the VAR. It is just the lack of consistency in the last few seasons.

"With the allowance of grappling, blocking, holding, I think we have lost a bit of what is a foul and what isn't a foul. Previously, it would be judged differently, so that's what upsets me."

It was a big call with huge ramifications.

It left Arsenal two wins from a first league title since 2004, with games to come at home to already-relegated Burnley and, in the final round, away to a Crystal Palace team that might field a weakened line-up due to having the Conference League final three days later.

It also left West Ham a point adrift of safety, in third-to-last place with two games remaining, while guaranteeing another season in the Premier League for Nottingham Forest and Leeds.

Fourth-to-last Tottenham can move four points clear of West Ham with a win over Leeds on Monday. It's now a two-team fight for survival between West Ham and Tottenham.

Leandro Trossard scored Arsenal's winning goal. (Getty Images: Arsenal FC/David Price)

AP/ABC

title: 'Time is now' for charging expansion as EV buzz builds
text:
South Australia's peak motoring body is calling for an urgent expansion of charging infrastructure as electric vehicle interest booms in the wake of the fuel crisis.

Sales of EVs have surged this year, with data from the Federal Chamber of Automotive Industries' VFACTS report showing EVs accounted for one in six new vehicles sold in April.

The RAA's Peter Nattrass said in recent busy periods, over Easter and during the AFL's Gather Round, chargers in regional areas such as the state's Limestone Coast were key to keeping people moving.

"We saw Mount Gambier, Bordertown and Tintinara were in the top 10 [chargers for usage]," he said.

"We're really seeing a lot of electric vehicle use in the regions, which shows that growing confidence people have now."

The RAA rolled out electric vehicle chargers across the state with funding from the state government. (Supplied: RAA)

But Mr Nattrass said with one in every two SA motorists considering an EV for their next vehicle, private investment supported by government was needed to expand the charging network.

"For every 1,000 electric vehicles added, there's another 17 charging points that are needed," he said.

"When we look at the concentrated demand in the south-east, the Riverland and up towards Port Augusta, there's a need to really build out that network.

"The RAA built out the minimum viable network needed statewide, but we really want to see a lot more choice in the market."

Chargers drawing visitors

Wattle Range Council chief executive Ben Gower switched to using an EV about three years ago.

"I've found it really easy to get around the state, around the region and particularly locally," he said.

Ben Gower says the council plans to add chargers in its office car park. (ABC South East SA: Josh Brine)

But Mr Gower said the council had received calls from the public for more chargers, with the council planning to build four charging stations at its Millicent office.

"It's going to be a mid-speed charging station, primarily to preserve council vehicle's batteries," he said.

"But it will also give an option for the community to get a charge in about an hour if they need it."

The RAA says for every 1,000 EVs, 17 charging places are needed. (ABC News: Jess Davis)

Mr Gower said developers were also looking at other locations in Millicent to install EV chargers.

"If you don't have a charging station they're going to keep travelling to the town that does have one," he said.

"I think the key is to at least have one fast charger in each of our significant regional towns."

Boosting fuel stocks

The RAA believes increasing charging infrastructure and making EVs more viable could also improve fuel security.

Mr Nattrass said increasing the number of electric cars would free up a large amount of fuel for other uses.

"Just [across] that 10-day Easter-Gather Round period, we estimate there was 560,000 litres of fuel freed up for other people [by electric car use]," he said.

The RAA believes EVs could save millions of litres of fuel. (ABC News: Daniel Taylor)

"Nationally, we have 500,000 electric vehicles on the road.

" We estimate that's [worth] 400 to 500 million litres of fuel that's available for other consumers. "

Mr Nattrass said it was vital for infrastructure to be built urgently to offer motorists more confidence to switch.

"The time is now for the government to support private investment into this sector," he said.

"There's a long lead time of 12 months, 18 months or more to build an EV charging point.

EVs accounted for one-sixth of new vehicle sales in April. (ABC News: Curtis Rodda)

"Right now is when we're needing to see state and federal support.

"If electric vehicles are part of our fuel security, we need to be seeing support particularly in tourism hotspots and major travel corridors."

The state government was contacted for comment.

title: Why including photos in your resume is a 'red flag' and other expert tips
text:
When it comes to resumes, it's not just about what to put in. What you leave out is important too.

Here's what three experts recommend you leave off your resume for the best chance of securing an interview or landing a role.

What not to include in a resume?

There's consensus between our three experts on whether to include a picture in a resume.

Their advice? Don't do it.

Natasha Hawker, the managing director of a human resources and recruitment service on Gadigal land in Sydney, says there are countries where it's considered mandatory, but in Australia, recruitment agencies will often remove them.

Natasha Hawker says clarity should be your "first priority" when writing and formatting a CV.

"Often those photos are not professional and believe me we've had all sorts."

Pictures, tables and graphs also make a resume harder for AI systems and recruiters to read, Ms Hawker says.

Similarly, Jon Gorgioski, the director of a resume consultancy practice in Naarm/Melbourne says photos and graphics don't generally belong on a resume.

And he says attaching other documents — such as university transcripts, reference letters and certificates — to your resume document is also unnecessary unless they are requested.

"Anything that creates distraction, privacy concerns, any bias or confusion probably doesn't belong on a [resume]."

This includes date of birth, marital status and full home address, Mr Gorgioski says.

Sign up to the ABC Lifestyle newsletter Get a mid-week boost and receive easy recipes, wellbeing ideas, and home and garden tips in your inbox every Wednesday. You’ll also receive a monthly newsletter of our best recipes.

Ms Hawker also says not to include the details of your referees and write "referees available upon request" instead.

"I am a referee for so many people [and] I do not want unsolicited phone calls when I'm not prepared for them."

Career coach Annique Teycheney, from Naarm/Melbourne, agrees, "unless it's specified in the job ad".

Applicants can then "word up" their references when they are asked to provide their contact, she says.

AI mistakes to avoid

People are increasingly using generative AI tools to help write resumes, but Ms Hawker says there are some common mistakes emerging as a result.

Some people forget to remove the parentheses or square brackets that often come with AI generated responses when they are copying and pasting them, she says.

The dos and don'ts of using AI for job applications Experts warn you should take a cautious approach when using generative artificial intelligence to apply for jobs.

Ms Hawker says to take the time to check and personalise responses and make sure you can speak to the information you've included in the resume if you do get an interview.

While AI can write professional content well, Mr Gorgioski says the content can also be too generic.

"That can become a bit of a red flag when we're reading something that doesn't actually tell me anything about how a person works, even though it may be written very well."

"Stronger resumes", he adds, "tend to show what happened because you were in [a] role".

Disparity between the positions listed on a resume and LinkedIn can also be seen as a red flag and recruiters will generally just move onto the next applicant, Mr Gorgioski says.

What about hobbies and volunteering roles?

Volunteering experience can be helpful when relevant, Ms Hawker says.

For example, having volunteered at the zoo or a dog shelter if the job related to animal welfare could move your resume to "the top of the pile", even if you have comparable skills to other applicants.

Annique Teycheney says to keep the contents of your resume as relevant and concise as possible. (Supplied: Annique Teycheney)

Mr Gorgioski says hobbies can be a helpful talking point or have a relevant link to the role.

"We try and find where there's some sort of a positive link to the work that they're applying for or where it adds a bit of texture around who they are."

Ms Teycheney says if a hobby or volunteering experience is directly related to the industry, it could be worth listing under experience instead.

Should I still include being house captain in my resume?

Ms Hawker says positions such as school captain are great to include if you're in the early stages of your career, but you shouldn't be relying on them when you have more experience under your belt.

The time frame for including these positions probably falls between five and 10 years, she says.

Mr Gorgioski says, "the relevance does fade over time" but if someone has recently left school it might be their strongest leadership example.

"My rule of thumb is, if an older achievement genuinely supports that person's story or career direction it may still have a place."

What to include and how to make your resume stand out

"We still see far too many resumes that are just thrown at the role," Ms Hawker says.

Mr Gorgioski adds you shouldn't have to write a new resume every time you apply for a job, but it should be tweaked or tailored to each position.

Ms Teycheney agrees that these are the resumes that stand out.

She also recommends getting someone else to look over it for you.

"Having someone skim their eyes over your resume for you to provide that feedback is worth its weight in gold."

title: Fowler one-ups Kerr to reach FA Cup final
text:
Mary Fowler scored the goal that kick-started Manchester City's fabulous late comeback to down Chelsea 3-2 and progress to the Women's FA Cup final.

Erin Cuthbert had given Chelsea an early lead before Matildas captain Sam Kerr added a second just before the hour mark to put the Blues in a comfortable position in front of a disappointing crowd of 7,773 at Stamford Bridge.

Kerr should have had an earlier goal too, but it was disallowed after the referee erroneously decided it had gone out of play before Ellie Carpenter could swing in the cross and had a third effort ruled out for offside.

That did not seem to matter though as the hosts dominated for 80 minutes of the match and were seemingly home and hosed on the path to Wembley.

But Manchester City had other ideas.

Fowler, only brought on a couple of minutes earlier as a sub, fired home a left-foot strike from 20 metres out in the 86th minute of the match — her first goal for City since February 2025 — to give hope where there had been none.

Star striker Khadija Shaw then did the rest, levelling with a fine volley on the turn in added time before heading home from Yui Hasegawa's cross in extra-time.

It means Kerr, despite scoring 19 goals in 17 matches in the FA Cup in her career, may bow out without a Wembley swansong should she move to America's NWSL with Denver next season.

Sam Kerr scored one but had two disallowed as Chelsea crashed out of the FA Cup. (Getty Images: Jasper Wax)

Manchester City's victory sets up a clash against Brighton and Hove Albion, led by former Socceroo Dario Vidošić.

Vidošić could become the first Australian manager to lead an English team to FA Cup glory after Brighton pulled off an amazing comeback of their own to reach the final with a 3-2 win at Liverpool.

The 39-year-old, who has carried on in England where he left off in Australia as Melbourne City's A-League Women-winning boss, saw Brighton battle back from 2-0 down after 22 minutes before going on to prevail with a stoppage-time clincher.

Liverpool felt they had been on course for the Wembley showdown on May 31 after first-half goals from Denise O'Sullivan and Beata Olsson.

Dario Vidošić could become Australia's first FA Cup-winning coach. (Getty Images: The FA/Kate McShane)

But Albion levelled through Manuela Vanegas and Madison Haley before Dutch midfielder Nadine Noordam, who had been on the pitch for only six minutes, lashed home her first goal for the club in the 95th minute.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Vidošić is the first Aussie manager to reach the final since Joe Montemurro last guided Arsenal to the 2021 showdown, but could go one better than the current Matildas coach who lost both his two Gunners' finals.

"It is a nice feeling when almost the last kick of the game hits the back of the net and sends us to Wembley," Vidošić said.

"The message to the girls will be, 'Enjoy this moment, they deserve this moment.'

"We want to continuously get better, and we want to go undefeated for the rest of the season."

Vidošić has done a remarkable job at the ambitious south-coast club, taking them to a record fifth-place in last year's Women's Super League, and having beaten both champions City, in the WSL, and European champions Arsenal, in the FA Cup, last month.

ABC/AAP

title: 'I wasn't weak': NSW Health worker wins $50k in discrimination case
text:
An admin officer has been awarded more than $50,000 in compensation after a tribunal found a NSW Health agency discriminated against her due to her disability.

Soneva Donald-Stanton has no function in one of her hands and limited mobility in her neck due to a musculoskeletal condition called Klippel Feil syndrome.

She had worked in administration for more than 14 years and said her condition had never interfered with her ability to perform her role.

But Ms Donald-Stanton said she became concerned about the way she was being treated by management several months after she started working at Western Sydney Local Health District's (WSLHD) Aboriginal Health Strategy department in December 2021.

Despite not requesting any assistance or modifications, she said she was referred for a workstation assessment and medical check after her manager raised concerns about her physical limitations and questioned her ability to complete office tasks.

Ms Donald-Stanton did not request any assistance, but was still referred for a medical check. (ABC News: Michael Donnelly)

Ms Donald-Stanton said the medical assessment was "very intrusive", lasted four hours and left her feeling "violated".

"It included a physical examination, it included me giving a lot of personal health information, it involved a cognitive test which I found extremely insulting," she said.

" It was just an all-round awful experience. "

The practitioner who conducted the assessment found Ms Donald-Stanton could only continue in her role if her workplace could accommodate a number of conditions.

These included modifications to her commute to the office and restrictions on colleagues talking to Ms Donald-Stanton about her disability, conditions which Ms Donald-Stanton was told could not be met.

She was subsequently declared unfit for work and placed on leave.

Apology, compensation ordered

Ms Donald-Stanton went on to pursue legal action against her employer, with the Civil and Administrative Tribunal in April finding she had been discriminated against.

In a statement to the tribunal, Ms Donald-Stanton's manager said she had been "concerned that there was more [the workplace] could be doing" to support the admin worker.

The manager said she was worried "without additional supports [WSLHD] may be exposing [Ms Donald-Stanton] to risks to her health and safety".

Ms Donald-Stanton says the medical assessment was intrusive. (Unsplash: Frederick Medina)

But the tribunal found her manager failed to identify why she thought Ms Donald-Stanton's health and safety was at risk at work.

"It was only because [her manager] observed that [Ms Donald-Stanton] had a physical limitation in her hand and in turning her head that she initiated the course of less favourable treatment," the tribunal found.

" [Ms Donald-Stanton's] disabilities clearly played a part in [her employer] treating her less favourably than the comparator in being deemed unfit for work and directed to take leave. "

WSLHD was ordered to apologise to Ms Donald-Stanton and provide all managers with disability discrimination training.

The tribunal found Ms Donald-Stanton should receive $55,000 in damages for the "hurt, humiliation and injury to feelings" she suffered in the workplace.

A WSLHD spokesperson said the agency was reviewing the tribunal findings.

"Western Sydney Local Health District is committed to providing a safe workplace and treating all employees with respect and fairness," the spokesperson said in a statement.

'I wasn't weak'

Ms Donald-Stanton says the decision is important for the disability community. (ABC News: Michael Donnelly)

Ms Donald-Stanton said her experience was the realisation of a long-held fear of being treated differently because of her physical disability.

"I experience it all the time, the assumption that you're incapable … that you're cognitively impaired because of the way you look," she said.

"I'm astounded that there are still these kinds of attitudes out there in the community.

" They judged the book by its cover with me, and I hope that me winning this case shows that I wasn't weak, I wasn't vulnerable. "

Ms Donald-Stanton said the findings marked a significant win for the disability community.

"There were a few things that kept me going [through the legal proceedings] and it was the fact that I did not want this to happen to anyone else and that we needed to move the needle in regards to how seriously it is taken and treated," she said.

"I honestly couldn't care less about the money, but it's the fact that it sends a clear signal to the employer that if you do not do the right thing, it will be a significant economic cost to you."

'Paternalistic' approach

Ms Donald-Stanton was represented by Women's Legal Service NSW during the proceedings, with assistant principal solicitor Sharmilla Bargon saying people with a disability were best-placed to understand their own capacity and workplace needs.

"Employers have to move away from a paternalistic, assumption-based approach, treating disability as an inherent vulnerability or risk," she said.

"Just having an employee with a disability doesn't give an employer permission to request an employee's full medical history, or broad intrusive health information.

"The case really reinforces a shift from a medical model of disability focused on perceived limitations or risk, to a human rights model centred on autonomy, dignity, inclusion and removing workplace barriers."

title: Woolworths worker told to cover his ‘plumber’s crack’ has case dismissed
text:
Advertisement

BusinessCompaniesUnfair dismissal Woolworths worker told to cover his ‘plumber’s crack’ has case dismissed Millie Muroi May 11, 2026 — 2:02pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

A Victorian man who said his feelings had been hurt after being told to cover up his “plumber’s crack” has been berated by the Fair Work Commission for filing an unfair dismissal case that wasted the workplace arbiter’s time. In a decision published last week, Fair Work Commission deputy president Alan Colman said he threw out a case filed by a Woolworths employee seeking compensation from the supermarket giant for a dismissal that never occurred. A casual employee at Woolworths said his feelings were hurt after being told to cover up his “plumber’s crack”. Louie Douvis “Anyone wanting insight into the phenomenon of unmeritorious claims in the Fair Work Commission may wish to consider [this case],” Colman said, noting such cases compounded the commission’s “burgeoning workload”. Earlier this year, Fair Work Commission president Justice Adam Hatcher said he expected close to 55,000 cases to come across the arbiter’s table this financial year, up 70 per cent in the space of three years. “There is no sign of this growth trend plateauing out, and we have no idea what the ‘new normal’ will be,” he said at the time, blaming applicants’ use of AI tools.

Advertisement

The worker who filed the case against Woolworths said he was told by a co-worker during a casual shift – in rude terms – to cover up his so-called “plumber’s crack” (or, as Colman described it, “the cleft of his bottom [which] was protruding from his trousers”). “[He] was upset,” Colman said. “His feelings were hurt. He lodged an application alleging that he had been dismissed in breach of his workplace rights.” Related Article Workplace A security guard was sacked for sleeping at work. Could it happen to you? However, Colman said Woolworths was not aware of any dismissal involving the man, and that he was never dismissed. “[Woolworths] said that [he] continued to work shifts after lodging his claim and that he later stopped turning up for work,” he said. “This case had nothing to do with dismissal. It was evidently a speculative claim made in pursuit of a monetary settlement that would spare Woolworths the nuisance of defending it.”

Advertisement

Colman said the man ignored his direction to attend the telephone hearing for the case and noted that this was the individual’s fifth application in two years. Cases such as this one are unfair, Colman pointed out because the claimant often has little to lose and because they use up the commission’s resources and waste time. “This is unfair to respondents who have no case to answer,” he said. “It is unfair to applicants with cases of substance waiting their turn to be heard. There is no effective disincentive for speculative claims, and so they come, in great numbers, compounding the commission’s burgeoning caseload.” The Business Briefing newsletter delivers major stories, exclusive coverage and expert opinion. title: Australia news LIVE: Polling data shows scale of One Nation’s win in Farrer; Government gears up to deliver federal budget; Iran responds to US’ newest ceasefire proposal
text:
Beijing has announced that US President Donald Trump will be hosted in the Chinese capital from May 13 to 15, finally confirming the state visit announced weeks ago by the White House.

Trump is expected to arrive in Beijing on Wednesday evening and is scheduled to hold talks with Chinese leader Xi Jinping on Thursday and Friday, according to details briefed out by the US side. It will be the first visit to China by a US president in nine years, with the last occasion by Trump in 2017 during his first term.

Donald Trump and Xi Jinping shake hands after their talks in South Korea in October last year. Getty

The summit was first flagged when the two leaders last met in Busan in South Korea last year, and was initially designed to address ongoing trade tensions. The Iran war is now set to be a major focus of the meetings, with Trump expected to press Xi for support in reopening the Strait of Hormuz. It comes after US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent last week accused China of “funding the largest state sponsor of terrorism” by purchasing 90 per cent of Iran’s oil.

The two sides are also expected to extend their trade truce, with speculation they may announce a board of trade and a board of investment to manage their differences. Washington wants China to purchase more Boeing airplanes, US beef and soybeans, while Beijing is expected to emphasise its claims to Taiwan.

Many analysts expect Xi to press Trump to scale back US weapons sales to the democratic island and change the US government’s official rhetorical stance to include opposing Taiwan’s independence.

title: The one kitchen gadget you need for a better chicken curry in half the time
text:
Using bone-in chicken gives this aromatic coconut curry a lift. Patricia Niven

This recipe, from my book What We Call Masala, isn’t traditional. But it is delicious. You can replace the thigh with curry-cut chicken, the gold standard among Indian butchers, in which the chicken is chopped into pieces, keeping the skin and bone intact.

It uses pungent and earthy spices. When using significant amounts of spice, the masala (spice blend) will need to be cooked through at different stages so as not to end up unbalanced or raw.

A pressure cooker is your best friend here, getting this on the table in under an hour. No pressure cooker? No worries – I’ve included stovetop instructions for those who don’t mind a longer simmer.

title: Olympio boosts Quebec gold stake to 51 per cent after bonanza hits
text:
